steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1 calls Asimov's flat characters a stylistic weakness. Text 2 pushes back, arguing the minimal characterization is intentional — it serves Asimov's theme that individuals are insignificant across historical epochs. The question asks what the student in Text 2 would say about Text 1's critique.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the flatness stems from a different cause than Text 1 claims': Text 2 does not dispute the cause so much as deny it is a flaw at all. This misframes the argument. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the critique applies only to some of Asimov's works': Text 2 makes no such scope limitation. ✗
  • Choice C — 'the flatness should not be considered a flaw': Exactly Text 2's position — the minimal characterization is a deliberate thematic choice, not a weakness. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Text 1 misidentifies which characters are flat': Text 2 does not dispute which characters are flat, only whether flatness is a flaw. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
